title: Khoresh fesenjan
subtitle: Persian walnut and pomegranate chicken stew
excerpt: Chicken thighs braised in a sauce of ground walnuts and pomegranate molasses until the sauce turns dark, thick, and richly sour-sweet. One of the great stews of the Persian kitchen. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Khoresh fesenjan is one of the classic khoreshes (stews) of Iranian cuisine, traditionally made with duck or chicken and served at celebration meals. Documented by Claudia Roden in The Food of Italy and the Eastern Mediterranean, by Najmieh Batmanglij in Food of Life: Ancient Persian and Modern Iranian Cooking (2011), and by Sabrina Ghayour in Persiana (2014). The balance between the sourness of pomegranate molasses and the bitterness of walnuts is the defining characteristic.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to three months. The sauce thickens further on freezing — loosen with a splash of water when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily and improves on the second day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better made a day ahead. The walnut sauce absorbs into the chicken overnight and the flavour deepens considerably.

content.text: Fesenjan is a stew that looks austere and tastes extraordinary. The sauce is built from ground walnuts and pomegranate molasses — nothing else gives it colour or flavour. As it cooks, the walnut oil disperses into the sauce and turns it from a murky brown paste into something dark, thick, and glossy, with a sour-sweet depth that cannot really be described in terms of other dishes.

The walnuts need to be ground fine — a food processor does this in 30 seconds. Coarsely chopped walnuts will not break down during cooking and produce a grainy sauce. The pomegranate molasses varies widely in sourness between brands; taste yours before adding it and adjust the quantity accordingly.

items.prepNote: ground fine in a food processor
taste your brand — adjust if very sour

content.text: Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a heavy casserole over medium-high heat. Season the chicken thighs with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and brown them in batches, skin side down, for 5 minutes per batch until deeply golden. Remove and set aside. Reduce the heat to medium. Cook {{onion}} finely chopped onion in the same pan for 8 minutes until soft. Add {{cinnamon-ground}} of ground cinnamon and {{cardamom-green}} cardamom pods; stir for 30 seconds. Add the ground walnuts and stir for 2 minutes until they start to toast and smell nutty. Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock and stir to combine. Add {{pomegranate-molasses}} of pomegranate molasses and stir well. Return the chicken to the pan, nestling it into the sauce. Cover and simmer over the lowest heat for 45 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce is thick and dark and the chicken is cooked through. Remove the cardamom pods. Taste and adjust with more pomegranate molasses or a pinch of salt. Scatter with pomegranate seeds if using. Serve with plain rice.

items.cause: Pomegranate molasses brand is very acidic.
items.fix: Add a teaspoon of honey or sugar and stir through. Taste again — the sweetness should balance the sourness without making it cloying.
The walnuts need to be ground almost to a powder in the food processor. If the sauce is grainy, blitz it with a stick blender in the pot.

items.cause: Heat too high during the long simmer.
items.fix: Keep the heat very low — a gentle blip rather than a rolling simmer. Stir occasionally and the sauce will come back together.

content.text: Fesenjan is a celebration dish in Iran, associated with weddings, Nowruz (Persian new year), and other occasions where the cook has time and inclination. The combination of ground walnuts and pomegranate molasses is ancient — both are native to the region and have been paired in cooking for centuries. The stew is served with steamed basmati rice, often with a tahdig (the crisp rice crust from the bottom of the pan) alongside. In Iranian home cooking, it is considered one of the most prestigious khoreshes, the category of rich, slow-cooked stews that defines the cuisine.
